title: Synthetic AU Abuse: Ghost Authorized User on Aged Thin-File Account
narrative: A synthetic identity with minimal credit history (2-4 months seasoning, $500-800 limit) suddenly adds an authorized user. Within 72 hours, both cards exhibit coordinated high-velocity activity across different merchant categories. The primary cardholder maintains low spend ($15-40 utilities, gas) while the AU card processes $200-450 transactions at electronics retailers, digital goods merchants (MCC 5816, 5732), and crypto on-ramps. The AU card shows distinct device fingerprints (different card1 values) but identical billing address (addr1 match, dist1=0). Total account utilization spikes from 15% to 92% within one week. Pattern peaks with simultaneous CNP purchases: primary at $38 streaming service, AU at $487 electronics, both within 4-minute window. Email domains differ (P_emaildomain: burner services like guerrillamail, R_emaildomain: synthetic match). Bust-out completes when both cards hit decline threshold, typically 8-12 days post-AU addition.
indicators: card1_txn_count_7d jumps from 2-3 to 18+ after AU addition within 72-hour window
Primary card avg transaction $22-45, AU card avg $285-520 same period
addr1 identical but card1, DeviceInfo, P_emaildomain all differ between primary/AU
Account utilization 15-25% baseline, spikes to 85-98% within 5-7 days of AU activation
AU card shows 3+ transactions at MCC 5732/5816 (electronics/software) within first 48 hours
Simultaneous transactions (txn_hour delta <8 minutes) from cards with dist1=0 but different DeviceInfo
Total account age 60-120 days at AU addition, previous monthly spend <$200
summary: A barely-used credit account suddenly adds an authorized user, then both cards rapidly rack up charges in different spending patterns—the original card stays small (utilities, gas under $50) while the new AU card hits electronics and crypto merchants for $200-500 transactions, all shipping to the same address but from different devices.
reasoning: Fraudsters layer a fake authorized user onto a synthetic identity they've been quietly aging, then coordinate a fast bust-out before the issuer catches on—the mismatched spending profiles and devices reveal two criminal operators draining credit they never intend to repay.
indicator_explanations: Transaction frequency explodes immediately after authorized user is added to account
New user spends ten times more per transaction than primary cardholder
Same billing address but different card, device, and email between users
Credit line suddenly maxed out within a week of adding user
New user immediately buys high-value electronics and digital goods
Two cards transact simultaneously from same location using different devices
Young account with minimal spending history suddenly adds authorized user
